summary: Jeff Atwood explores Tim Berners-Lee's Principle of Least Power, which argues that choosing less powerful languages makes data more accessible and analyzable. The post contrasts simple declarative formats (which anyone can parse and analyze) with powerful but opaque solutions like Java applets (which only humans can interpret). Atwood uses this principle to introduce his famous 'Atwood's Law': any application that can be written in JavaScript will eventually be written in JavaScript. The post recommends Berners-Lee's broader architectural writings as containing timeless web design wisdom.
key_points: Computer science has spent decades maximizing language power, but we now need to appreciate choosing the least powerful solution
Less powerful, declarative formats enable data to be retrieved, analyzed, averaged, plotted, and combined with other information
Powerful solutions like Java applets may offer cool interfaces but are completely opaque to search engines and automated analysis
Atwood's Law: any application that can be written in JavaScript will eventually be written in JavaScript
money_quotes: Computer Science spent the last forty years making languages which were as powerful as possible. Nowadays we have to appreciate the reasons for picking not the most powerful solution but the least powerful.
The less powerful the language, the more you can do with the data stored in that language.
The only way to find out what a Java applet means is to set it running in front of a person.
Any application that can be written in JavaScript, will eventually be written in JavaScript.
key_insight: Choosing less powerful, more declarative solutions makes your data universally accessible and analyzable, while overly powerful solutions create opaque black boxes that only humans can interpret.
